result.markdown:
--- title: Data Report --- All information on the data used in the project is compiled here to ensure traceability and reproducibility of results. The project is based on a single public dataset covering global cybersecurity incidents from 2015 to 2024. The dataset requires no complex merging or feature engineering; this report therefore focuses on the raw dataset characteristics and the light normalisation applied during loading. ## Raw data ### Overview Raw Datasets : Overview of raw datasets used in the project. {#tbl-raw-overview} | Name | Source | Storage location | |---|---|---| | Global Cybersecurity Threats (2015–2024) | Kaggle — atharvasoundankar/global-cybersecurity-threats-2015-2024 | `data/raw/Global_Cybersecurity_Threats_2015-2024.csv` | ### Details: Global Cybersecurity Threats (2015–2024) - **Description**: 3,000 simulated cybersecurity incidents recorded between 2015 and 2024 across ten countries and seven target industries. Each row represents one incident and includes contextual, operational, and outcome variables: country, year, attack type, target industry, financial loss, number of affected users, attack source, exploited vulnerability type, defense mechanism used, and incident resolution time. - **Data source / provider**: Published on Kaggle by user *atharvasoundankar*. Dataset URL: [https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/atharvasoundankar/global-cybersecurity-threats-2015-2024](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/atharvasoundankar/global-cybersecurity-threats-2015-2024) - **Data procurement**: Download via the Kaggle API using `data_acquisition/load.py`. Run `python data_acquisition/load.py` from the project root; the script authenticates with Kaggle (requires `KAGGLE_USERNAME` and `KAGGLE_KEY` in `.env`), downloads, and saves the file to `data/raw/`. Any team member with valid Kaggle credentials can reproduce the acquisition independently. - **Legal aspects**: Licensed under the Kaggle dataset terms (CC0 / public domain). The data is synthetic / aggregated and does not contain personally identifiable information. Suitable for educational and non-commercial use without restrictions. - **Data governance**: Public data. No personal, business-sensitive, or confidential information is present. No special handling or anonymisation is required. - **Variable classification**: The dataset is used for descriptive and comparative visualisation only. There is no modelling target variable. `financial_loss_in_million_`, `number_of_affected_users`, and `incident_resolution_time_in_hours` serve as outcome / severity measures. All remaining columns are contextual or categorical grouping variables. #### Data Catalogue : Data catalogue for Global Cybersecurity Threats (2015–2024). {#tbl-data-catalogue} | Column index | Column name (raw) | Normalised name | Datatype | Values / range | Short description | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Country | `country` | string | Australia, Brazil, China, France, Germany, India, Japan, Russia, UK, USA | Country where the incident was recorded | | 2 | Year | `year` | int | 2015–2024 | Year of the incident | | 3 | Attack Type | `attack_type` | string | DDoS, Malware, Man-in-the-Middle, Phishing, Ransomware, SQL Injection | Type of cyberattack | | 4 | Target Industry | `target_industry` | string | Banking, Education, Government, Healthcare, IT, Retail, Telecommunications | Industry sector targeted by the attack | | 5 | Financial Loss (in Million $) | `financial_loss_in_million_` | float | Positive values (USD millions) | Estimated financial loss associated with the incident | | 6 | Number of Affected Users | `number_of_affected_users` | int | Positive integers | Number of users impacted by the incident | | 7 | Attack Source | `attack_source` | string | Hacker Group, Insider Threat, Nation-state, Unknown | Attributed source or actor behind the attack | | 8 | Security Vulnerability Type | `security_vulnerability_type` | string | Misconfiguration, Social Engineering, Third-party Vendor, Unpatched Software, Weak Passwords, Zero-day Exploit | Type of vulnerability exploited | | 9 | Defense Mechanism Used | `defense_mechanism_used` | string | AI-based Detection, Antivirus, Employee Training, Encryption, Firewall, Intrusion Detection System, Multi-factor Authentication, VPN | Primary defense mechanism in place at the time of the incident | | 10 | Incident Resolution Time (in Hours) | `incident_resolution_time_in_hours` | int | Positive integers | Time taken to resolve the incident, in hours | #### Data Characteristics and Quality The exploratory analysis uses the full dataset of 3,000 incidents. All code is folded by default; click to expand. ##### Summary Statistics ::: {#tbl-summary-stats .cell tbl-cap='Descriptive statistics for numerical variables.' execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} df[NUMERIC_COLS].describe().round(2).rename(columns={ "financial_loss_in_million_": "Financial Loss (M$)", "number_of_affected_users": "Affected Users", "incident_resolution_time_in_hours": "Resolution Time (h)" }) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
Financial Loss (M$) Affected Users Resolution Time (h)
count 3000.00 3000.00 3000.00
mean 50.49 504684.14 36.48
std 28.79 289944.08 20.57
min 0.50 424.00 1.00
25% 25.76 255805.25 19.00
50% 50.80 504513.00 37.00
75% 75.63 758088.50 55.00
max 99.99 999635.00 72.00
``` ::: ::: ##### Missing Values ::: {#cell-fig-missing .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} missing = df.isnull().mean().sort_values(ascending=False) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.5)) colors = ["#e8002d" if v > 0.05 else "#4C78A8" for v in missing.values] missing.plot.barh(ax=ax, color=colors) ax.set_xlabel("Fraction missing") ax.set_title("Missing values per column") ax.axvline(x=0.05, color="grey", linestyle="--", linewidth=0.8, label="5 % threshold") ax.legend() ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False) ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Fraction of missing values per column. No column exceeds the 5% threshold.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-missing-output-1.png){#fig-missing width=852 height=326} ::: ::: The dataset contains **no missing values** in any column. No imputation or row removal is required. ##### Distributions of Numerical Variables ::: {#cell-fig-num-dist .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} labels = { "financial_loss_in_million_": "Financial Loss (M$)", "number_of_affected_users": "Affected Users", "incident_resolution_time_in_hours": "Resolution Time (h)" } fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4)) for ax, col in zip(axes, NUMERIC_COLS): ax.hist(df[col], bins=40, color="#4C78A8", edgecolor="white", alpha=0.85) ax.axvline(df[col].median(), color="black", linestyle="--", linewidth=1, label=f"Median: {df[col].median():.1f}") ax.set_xlabel(labels[col]) ax.set_ylabel("Count") ax.set_title(labels[col]) ax.legend(fontsize=8) ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False) ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Histograms of the three numerical outcome variables.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-num-dist-output-1.png){#fig-num-dist width=1238 height=374} ::: ::: ::: {#cell-fig-boxplots .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4)) for ax, col in zip(axes, NUMERIC_COLS): ax.boxplot(df[col].dropna(), vert=True, patch_artist=True, boxprops=dict(facecolor="#4C78A8", color="#4C78A8", alpha=0.6), medianprops=dict(color="black", linewidth=2), flierprops=dict(marker="o", markersize=2, alpha=0.3, color="#4C78A8")) ax.set_ylabel(labels[col]) ax.set_title(labels[col]) ax.set_xticks([]) ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False) ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Box plots of numerical outcome variables (outliers shown as points).](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-boxplots-output-1.png){#fig-boxplots width=1238 height=374} ::: ::: All three numerical variables show a **broadly uniform distribution** across their respective ranges, which is consistent with the dataset being synthetically generated. There are no extreme outliers and the distributions appear roughly flat with no heavy skew. ##### Distributions of Categorical Variables ::: {#cell-fig-cat-dist .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} cat_labels = { "country": "Country", "attack_type": "Attack Type", "target_industry": "Target Industry", "attack_source": "Attack Source", "security_vulnerability_type": "Vulnerability Type", "defense_mechanism_used": "Defense Mechanism" } fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(15, 9)) for ax, col in zip(axes.flat, CAT_COLS): counts = df[col].value_counts().sort_values() counts.plot.barh(ax=ax, color="#4C78A8", edgecolor="white") ax.set_title(cat_labels[col]) ax.set_xlabel("Count") ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False) ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False) for bar, val in zip(ax.patches, counts.values): ax.text(bar.get_width() + 5, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height() / 2, str(val), va="center", fontsize=8) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Value counts for each categorical variable.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-cat-dist-output-1.png){#fig-cat-dist width=1429 height=854} ::: ::: All categorical variables are **evenly distributed** across their categories, again consistent with a balanced synthetic dataset. No single country, industry, or attack type dominates the data in a way that would cause representational issues in the visualisation. ##### Year Distribution ::: {#cell-fig-year-dist .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} year_counts = df["year"].value_counts().sort_index() fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.5)) year_counts.plot.bar(ax=ax, color="#4C78A8", edgecolor="white") ax.set_xlabel("Year") ax.set_ylabel("Number of incidents") ax.set_title("Incidents per Year") ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=0) ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False) ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False) for bar, val in zip(ax.patches, year_counts.values): ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height() + 2, str(val), ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=8) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Number of recorded incidents per year (2015–2024).](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-year-dist-output-1.png){#fig-year-dist width=758 height=326} ::: ::: ##### Correlations Between Numerical Variables ::: {#cell-fig-corr .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} corr = df[NUMERIC_COLS].corr(method="pearson") corr.index = ["Financial Loss", "Affected Users", "Resolution Time"] corr.columns = ["Financial Loss", "Affected Users", "Resolution Time"] fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4)) sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="RdBu_r", center=0, square=True, ax=ax, linewidths=0.5, vmin=-1, vmax=1) ax.set_title("Pearson Correlation — Numerical Variables") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Pearson correlation heatmap of the three numerical outcome variables.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-corr-output-1.png){#fig-corr width=431 height=373} ::: ::: ::: {#cell-fig-pairplot .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} col_labels = { "financial_loss_in_million_": "Financial Loss (M$)", "number_of_affected_users": "Affected Users", "incident_resolution_time_in_hours": "Resolution Time (h)" } fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 4)) for ax, col in zip(axes, NUMERIC_COLS): order = df.groupby("attack_type")[col].median().sort_values(ascending=False).index sns.boxplot(data=df, x="attack_type", y=col, order=order, ax=ax, color="#4C78A8", flierprops=dict(marker="o", markersize=2, alpha=0.3)) ax.set_xlabel("") ax.set_ylabel(col_labels[col]) ax.set_title(col_labels[col]) ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=25) ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False) ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False) fig.suptitle("Numerical Outcomes by Attack Type", y=1.02) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Distribution of each numerical outcome variable across attack types.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-pairplot-output-1.png){#fig-pairplot width=1429 height=396} ::: ::: The three numerical outcome variables show **near-zero correlations** with each other, indicating they capture largely independent dimensions of incident severity. Financial loss, affected users, and resolution time can therefore be used as complementary severity measures in the visualisation rather than as redundant representations of the same underlying signal. ##### YData Profiling — Full Report ::: {#profiling .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} from ydata_profiling import ProfileReport eda_dir = Path.cwd() / "eda" eda_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) profile = ProfileReport( df, title="Global Cybersecurity Threats 2015–2024", minimal=True, progress_bar=False, ) profile.to_file(eda_dir / "cybersecurity_profile_report.html") ``` ::: [Open full interactive profiling report](eda/cybersecurity_profile_report.html){target="_blank"} ##### Implications for Visualisation Design Based on the exploratory analysis, the following observations are relevant for the visualisation design and implementation: 1. **No missing values**: The dataset is complete. No indicators for missing data or imputation strategies are needed in the visualisation. 2. **Uniform distributions**: Both numerical and categorical variables are evenly distributed. This means no single category dominates the data, which supports fair visual comparisons across countries, industries, attack types, and other dimensions. 3. **Independent severity dimensions**: Financial loss, affected users, and resolution time are uncorrelated, confirming that they measure distinct aspects of incident severity. The visualisation should allow users to switch between these measures rather than combining them into a single metric. 4. **Consistent category counts**: All categorical variables have balanced representation, making sorted bar charts and heatmaps reliable without requiring special handling for sparse categories. 5. **Balanced year coverage**: Approximately 300 incidents per year across all ten years. Trend charts over time will show stable series without abrupt gaps or volume spikes caused by data density differences. 6. **Synthetic data limitations**: The uniform distributions and perfect balance across categories are consistent with a synthetically generated dataset. This limits the strength of causal or comparative claims. All findings should be framed as patterns within the dataset rather than conclusions about real-world cybersecurity trends. ## Processed Data ### Overview Processed Datasets : Overview of processed datasets used in the project. {#tbl-processed-overview} | Name | Source | Storage location | |---|---|---| | Normalised incidents | `data_acquisition/load.py` — column normalisation of the raw CSV | In-memory (loaded at render time) | ### Details: Normalised Incidents - **Description**: The raw dataset with column names normalised to lowercase snake_case for consistent programmatic access. No rows are added, removed, or aggregated. All values remain unchanged. - **Processing steps**: 1. Read `data/raw/Global_Cybersecurity_Threats_2015-2024.csv` with `pd.read_csv()` 2. Strip whitespace, lowercase, replace spaces and `/` with `_`, remove remaining non-word characters from all column names 3. Return the DataFrame as-is - **Access**: Call `load()` from `data_acquisition/load.py`. The function downloads the raw file automatically if it is not present. #### Data Catalogue The normalised column names are documented in the raw data catalogue above (@tbl-data-catalogue). No new columns are derived and no columns are removed. #### Data Characteristics and Quality ::: {#tbl-processed-quality .cell tbl-cap='Row and column counts before and after normalisation.' execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} summary = pd.DataFrame({ "": ["Raw dataset", "Normalised dataset"], "Rows": [len(df), len(df)], "Columns": [10, 10], "Missing values": [df.isnull().sum().sum(), df.isnull().sum().sum()], "Column names changed": ["No", "Yes (lowercase snake_case)"], }) summary.set_index("", inplace=True) summary ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ```{=html}
Rows Columns Missing values Column names changed
Raw dataset 3000 10 0 No
Normalised dataset 3000 10 0 Yes (lowercase snake_case)
``` ::: ::: Processing introduces no data loss. The normalisation step only affects column name formatting and does not alter any data values or row count.
